title: 1. Generous study leave: EY provides ample time off for SAICA board exam preparation, which is a major benefit for trainees. 2. Opportunity to build strong friendships: Despite the challenges, the shared experience fosters close bonds among colleagues. 3. Exposure to high-profile clients: Working on large engagements can accelerate learning and professional growth. 4. Flexible work arrangements for some: Managers often have more autonomy over their schedules, though this is not consistent across teams.
pros:
1. Generous study leave: EY provides ample time off for SAICA board exam preparation, which is a major benefit for trainees.
2. Opportunity to build strong friendships: Despite the challenges, the shared experience fosters close bonds among colleagues.
3. Exposure to high-profile clients: Working on large engagements can accelerate learning and professional growth.
4. Flexible work arrangements for some: Managers often have more autonomy over their schedules, though this is not consistent across teams.
cons:
1. Trainee quality and readiness: Many trainees lack basic computer literacy and technical knowledge, which places additional strain on teams and affects audit quality.
2. Toxic culture and poor morale: The environment is often hostile, especially at assistant manager level and above, with widespread dissatisfaction.
3. Lack of compassion from senior management: Personal circumstances are frequently disregarded, and excessive overtime is expected without empathy.
4. Unfair workload distribution: Management working on demanding clients with 7-day weeks receive the same pay as those on lighter engagements.
5. Underpayment across levels: Compensation is below industry standards, especially considering the workload and responsibilities.
6. HR issues: HR is often dismissive, makes inappropriate comments, and fails to act on bullying or harassment complaints.
7. HR Broken promises: Commitments around contracts, client exposure, and career development are frequently delayed or unfulfilled.
8. Retaliation for reporting concerns: Raising issues through formal channels can lead to targeting and negative treatment.
9. Inconsistent flexibility: While some managers enjoy flexible hours, others enforce rigid schedules regardless of role or performance.
10. Lack of support during illness: Being hospitalized or ill can result in unfair criticism and blame, depending on the manager.
11. High turnover and understaffing: Poor retention leads to knowledge gaps and increased pressure on remaining staff.

You're constantly learning new skills, from technical tools to soft skills like client management and presentation. The variety of projects means you'll never get bored and will become a more well-rounded professional in a short amount of time. * Strong Brand and Networking: The EY name on your resume is a huge asset. The company's global network and alumni connections open up doors that would otherwise be difficult to access. You get to work with and learn from some incredibly smart and driven people, both colleagues and clients. * Exposure to Diverse Industries: As a business analyst, you're not stuck in one industry. You can work on projects for a tech startup one month and a global financial institution the next. This broad exposure helps you understand different business models and challenges, which is great for building a versatile skill set. * Competitive Compensation: The salary and benefits package is generally strong. While the long hours are a reality, the compensation is commensurate with the demanding work environment, and there are good opportunities for performance-based bonuses and raises.

It can be difficult to maintain a good work-life balance, and burnout is a real risk. This is the nature of the consulting industry, but it's something to be prepared for.
 * Team-Dependent Culture: While the overall culture is positive, your day-to-day experience is heavily influenced by the specific team and manager you work with. A great team can make a challenging project feel manageable, while a less supportive one can make things a lot harder.
 * Pressure to Perform: There is a constant pressure to deliver high-quality work, often under tight deadlines. While this can be a motivator, it can also be stressful. You are expected to quickly get up to speed on new clients and projects, and the learning curve is steep.

title: - Benefits - Most people are hard working and know their stuff - Working with clients who have a great mission or outcome to Australia - Some amazing leaders - Compensation is good
pros:
- Benefits
- Most people are hard working and know their stuff
- Working with clients who have a great mission or outcome to Australia
- Some amazing leaders
- Compensation is good
cons:
- The dollar is still above all else
- Benefits are only good if you can actually use them without the fear of it impacting your role.
- Lots of talk from leaders but very little actual action. Most of the work to do from EY leaders great ideas is lumped onto the SM’s or M’s from an implementation standpoint whilst they also have to maintain ridiculously high util and sales targets. 
- EY Australia Leadership team say one thing but sub service line leaders, location leaders or your direct leaders may have their own agenda you must keep all happy to succeed. You are constantly being told we need to transform for the future, however don’t lose focus on the right now for dollars and utilisation of course.  Oh and please don’t forget we also want you to be the change in your teams to drive our vision forward so please whilst you already have a huge workload we need you to take on more to prove you are worthy of a nominal pay bump. 
- This weight of expectation is no more highly acute then at the SM level from what I observed. This is because your are often being expected to have up to 80% utilisation whilst also doing the majority of the actual day to day team management, sales generation and transforming for the future. You also don’t get approved to go to any events or training because the company can’t afford it whilst also seeing partners jet setting around the country or doing international conferences and expecting you to hold down the fort or chase up their leads and write the proposals. The system is broken and it in turn breaks people especially at that middle career point of SM and M who are bearing the weight of expectation from both sides but have to hustle for every bit of utilisation because we are not margin efficient so you have to pick up 1 or 2 days across 3 or 4 jobs just to hit your KPI’s.
- Taint on the industry from the PWC fall out is a hard pill to swallow amongst everything else. You work incredibly hard to feel proud of what you do just to feel dirty by association because of failings in the industry and within some sections of your own company.

title: Typical benefits of a consulting firm 1. Personal growth - you will learn A LOT. Client management, how to run projects, how to manage risks and issues, presentation skills. Your team & your bosses will guide you every step of the way. Everything you do (especially if you are junior) will be reviewed by a senior, a manager, a director and in some cases, a partner. 2. Competitive increment - if you are not incompetent and show signs of growth, you will definitely get a grade bump (your grade will increase e.g. from SA 2 to SA 3) and the progression comes with a really good increment (20-30%). Promotion from SA to manager is slightly harder - but if you're not incompetent and meet/exceed expectations, it's definitely easier than other companies. Thats why you will meet 26-27 year old managers (and a lot of them are very smart and competent)
pros:
Typical benefits of a consulting firm

1. Personal growth - you will learn A LOT. Client management, how to run projects, how to manage risks and issues, presentation skills. Your team & your bosses will guide you every step of the way. Everything you do (especially if you are junior) will be reviewed by a senior, a manager, a director and in some cases, a partner.  

2. Competitive increment - if you are not incompetent and show signs of growth, you will definitely get a grade bump (your grade will increase e.g. from SA 2 to SA 3) and the progression comes with a really good increment (20-30%). Promotion from SA to manager is slightly harder - but if you're not incompetent and meet/exceed expectations, it's definitely easier than other companies. Thats why you will meet 26-27 year old managers (and a lot of them are very smart and competent)
cons:
1. No WLB. Going home at 7pm is early. Taking leave can be difficult. Holidays need to be planned a year ahead. 

2. You will not have a lot of (if any) control what you will be specializing in. Depending on what clients / projects require resources, you will be thrown into those projects. You're not interested in the industry / project? Too bad so sad. 

3. Depending on your team - bosses can be relentless. Some bosses will not be kind to you for making mistakes. Can be disheartening getting yelled at / insulted for presentation slide that can be fixed in 15 mins.
4. It is a client-facing role - ultimately the client makes the decision. You will find yourself doing things that sometimes don't make business sense. But it's what the client wants! 

5. Some teams will speak in Mandarin during meetings, etc. May be exclusionary for those who can't speak Mandarin.

6. Bonus is really bad.
